interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced clopidogrel activation and antiplatelet effect, increased risk of cardiovascular events (stent thrombosis)
interactions.management: Use pantoprazole (least CYP2C19 inhibition) instead of omeprazole. Avoid omeprazole and esomeprazole with clopidogrel.

interactions.mechanism: Divalent and trivalent cations (Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Fe2+) form insoluble chelation complexes with fluoroquinolones in the GI tract.
interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced fluoroquinolone absorption by up to 90%, leading to treatment failure
interactions.management: Take fluoroquinolone 2 hours before or 6 hours after calcium-containing products.

interactions.mechanism: Iron (Fe2+/Fe3+) forms insoluble chelation complexes with fluoroquinolones, dramatically reducing antibiotic absorption.
interactions.clinical_effect: Reduced fluoroquinolone absorption by up to 75%, risk of antibiotic treatment failure
interactions.management: Take fluoroquinolone at least 2 hours before or 6 hours after iron supplements.

interactions.mechanism: Ivermectin may inhibit CYP2C9 or displace warfarin from protein binding, leading to increased free warfarin levels.
interactions.clinical_effect: Increased INR and potential bleeding risk
interactions.management: Monitor INR closely during and after ivermectin course. Adjust warfarin dose as needed.

interactions.mechanism: Dexamethasone inhibits CYP-mediated metabolism of albendazole sulfoxide (active metabolite), increasing its plasma levels by 50%.
interactions.clinical_effect: Increased albendazole sulfoxide levels. This is sometimes intentionally exploited in neurocysticercosis treatment.
interactions.management: In neurocysticercosis, this interaction is often therapeutically beneficial. In other settings, monitor for increased albendazole side effects.

interactions.mechanism: N-acetylcysteine may potentiate the vasodilatory effects of nitroglycerin by replenishing sulfhydryl groups needed for nitrate bioactivation.
interactions.clinical_effect: Enhanced hypotensive effect and headache. May also improve nitrate tolerance.
interactions.management: Monitor blood pressure closely. May need to reduce nitroglycerin dose. Be prepared for more pronounced hypotension.

interactions.mechanism: Both propofol and fentanyl cause dose-dependent cardiorespiratory depression through different mechanisms (GABA-A and mu-opioid, respectively).
interactions.clinical_effect: Additive cardiorespiratory depression: hypotension, bradycardia, respiratory depression, apnea
interactions.management: Standard in anesthesia but requires careful titration. Reduce propofol induction dose by 20-30% when fentanyl is co-administered.

interactions.mechanism: Ketoconazole potently inhibits CYP3A4, which is the sole metabolic pathway for midazolam, increasing its AUC by 10-15 fold.
interactions.clinical_effect: Dramatically prolonged and intensified sedation, respiratory depression, and potential coma
interactions.management: Avoid oral midazolam with ketoconazole. If IV midazolam needed, reduce dose by 75% and monitor in ICU setting.

interactions.mechanism: Both ketamine and theophylline lower the seizure threshold through different mechanisms. Theophylline antagonizes adenosine; ketamine affects NMDA receptors.
interactions.clinical_effect: Significantly reduced seizure threshold with risk of tonic-clonic seizures during anesthesia
interactions.management: Avoid ketamine in patients on theophylline. Use alternative anesthetic agent. If unavoidable, have anticonvulsant ready.

interactions.mechanism: Both lidocaine and amiodarone depress cardiac conduction and contractility. Amiodarone also inhibits CYP3A4, reducing lidocaine metabolism.
interactions.clinical_effect: Additive cardiac depression: bradycardia, heart block, cardiac arrest, and lidocaine toxicity (seizures)
interactions.management: Use combination with extreme caution. Reduce lidocaine dose. Continuous cardiac monitoring required.

interactions.mechanism: Vitamin K is the pharmacological antidote to warfarin. It restores synthesis of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X).
interactions.clinical_effect: Reversal of anticoagulant effect. Therapeutic use for warfarin over-anticoagulation or bleeding. Dietary vitamin K causes INR fluctuations.
interactions.management: For reversal: IV vitamin K 1-10mg based on INR and bleeding. For dietary: maintain consistent vitamin K intake. Do not abruptly change intake.

interactions.mechanism: Protamine is a strongly positively charged protein that binds to negatively charged heparin, forming an inactive stable complex and neutralizing anticoagulant activity.
interactions.clinical_effect: Reversal of heparin anticoagulation (1mg protamine neutralizes ~100 units UFH). Intentional therapeutic use.
interactions.management: Dose based on amount of heparin given and time elapsed. Give slowly IV (max 50mg over 10 min). Monitor for protamine reactions.
